title: Dog Black Crusty Warts on Skin: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out what black crusty warts on your dog might be, the urgency level of these growths, and how photos help your vet provide a diagnosis.
content_html:
What are these black, crusty growths?

Finding a new lump or bump on your pet can be alarming. When owners describe "black crusty warts," they are often seeing sebaceous adenomas or old-age warts. These are typically benign growths originating from the oil glands in the skin. Over time, the surface can become pigmented (turning black) and develop a dry, crusty texture due to exposure to the air or minor irritation. While they may look unsightly, they are common in aging dogs and are often harmless skin changes.

Is this a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for black crusty warts is generally Low. In most cases, these growths do not require immediate after-hours care. However, you should schedule a routine appointment if the growth begins to bleed frequently, shows signs of infection like pus or a foul odor, or if your dog is constantly licking and chewing at it. If the growth is rapidly changing in size or shape, it is best to have it evaluated by a veterinarian sooner rather than later to rule out more serious conditions like melanoma.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing your veterinarian with a clear, high-resolution photo can significantly speed up the triage process. A good photo allows the medical team to see the exact location, texture, and color of the growth before you even step into the office. Because some growths can change appearance when a dog is stressed or moving during an exam, a photo taken at home provides a stable reference point for the vet to monitor growth over time.

faqs.question: Can I use human wart remover on my dog?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use over-the-counter human wart medications on dogs. These chemicals can be toxic if licked and can cause severe chemical burns on canine skin.

faqs.question: Are black warts on dogs contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Most black, crusty warts in older dogs are sebaceous adenomas, which are not contagious. However, viral papillomas can be spread between dogs, though they usually appear differently and are not contagious to humans.

faqs.question: Will these warts go away on their own?
faqs.answer: While viral papillomas in young dogs often resolve as the immune system matures, sebaceous adenomas and age-related warts usually persist and may slowly grow over time unless surgically removed.
